source.title: Accelerated enzyme engineering by machine-learning guided cell-free expression

initial_request.research_problem: Enzyme engineering is limited by low-throughput directed evolution that misses epistatic interactions and focuses on single transformations.
initial_request.known_context: Traditional directed evolution methods are low-throughput and often miss epistatic interactions. Selection methods focus on single transformations, limiting the collection of positive and negative sequence-function relationships.
constraints: Must handle multiple distinct chemical reactions
quantifiable_goals: Improve activity over wild-type McbA for multiple amide products
Demonstrate ML-guided platform can outperform traditional ISM

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Enzyme engineering is limited by low-throughput directed evolution that misses epistatic interactions and focuses on single transformations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to rapidly generate and use large datasets of sequence-function relationships for predictive design.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A high-throughput, ML-guided platform integrating cell-free DNA assembly, cell-free gene expression, and functional assays can rapidly map fitness landscapes and optimize enzymes for multiple reactions.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop an ML-guided cell-free platform to address the limitations of traditional directed evolution.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The platform concept is proposed to overcome low throughput and capture epistatic interactions.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to explore the reaction space of McbA to identify target reactions for engineering.

evidence.quote_or_span: Enzyme engineering is limited by the challenge of rapidly generating and using large datasets of sequence-function relationships for predictive design.
screening strategies are generally low throughput, which constrains resampling mutations in iterative site saturation mutagenesis campaigns and can miss epistatic interactions
selection methods for directed evolution focus on “winning” enzymes for a single transformation, which limits the ability to collect positive and negative sequence-function relationships

we develop a machine learning (ML)-guided platform that integrates cell-free DNA assembly, cell-free gene expression, and functional assays to rapidly map fitness landscapes across protein sequence space and optimize enzymes for multiple, distinct chemical reactions.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis of ML-guided cell-free platform proposed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which amidation reactions can McbA catalyze, and which should be targeted for engineering?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: McbA has broad substrate promiscuity that can be exploited for pharmaceutical amide synthesis.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Explore the amidation reaction space of wild-type McbA by evaluating enzymatic substrate promiscuity with 1100 unique reactions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: McbA showed activity on a wide range of substrates, identifying target reactions for engineering.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to implement a cell-free workflow to rapidly generate mutant libraries for the identified targets.

evidence.quote_or_span: We apply this platform to engineer amide synthetases by evaluating substrate preference for 1217 enzyme variants in 10,953 unique reactions.

Through efforts to build ML models and all ISM rounds, we explored the sequence-function landscape of McbA by assessing 2856 variants of McbA (1217 variants of which were for ML models), 1100 possible amide products, and 12,584 substrate pair-mutant reactions.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Target reactions identified from McbA substrate promiscuity screen.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to rapidly generate sequence-defined protein libraries for McbA without laborious cloning?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Cell-free DNA assembly and cell-free gene expression can streamline mutant library generation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Implement a cell-free protein synthesis workflow to rapidly generate sequence-defined protein libraries for McbA.

tool_or_method.name: Cell-free DNA assembly and gene expression

evolution_trajectory.observation: Cell-free workflow successfully generated McbA mutant libraries, avoiding laborious cloning steps.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: With the library generation method in place, conduct a hot spot screen of single mutants for initial target reactions.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Cell-free workflow established for mutant library generation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which single mutations are beneficial for the target reactions moclobemide, metoclopramide, and cinchocaine?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A hot spot screen of single mutants across key residues will identify beneficial mutations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Conduct hot spot screen (HSS) of 1216 single mutants across 64 residues for three target reactions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Multiple residues with positive impact on each target reaction were identified.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: In parallel, run traditional ISM campaigns for benchmarking and to generate higher-order mutants.

evidence.quote_or_span: Our HSS of these residues (64 residues × 19 amino acids = 1216 total single mutants) revealed multiple residues that had a positive impact on moclobemide (Fig.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: HSS data collected for three target reactions.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How do traditional ISM campaigns perform on these targets, and can they provide higher-order mutants for benchmarking?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ISM can iteratively accumulate beneficial mutations to generate improved variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Run traditional iterative saturation mutagenesis (ISM) campaigns for moclobemide, metoclopramide, and cinchocaine.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ISM campaigns for moclobemide and metoclopramide yielded higher-order mutants with improved activity, but cinchocaine campaign failed to find beneficial mutations beyond a double mutant.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: The failure for cinchocaine motivates the need for ML models to capture epistatic interactions.

evidence.quote_or_span: In parallel, we conducted a more traditional directed evolution campaign on each amide product (moclobemide, metoclopramide, and cinchocaine) via iterative saturation mutagenesis (ISM).
This would provide valuable higher order mutations to validate and benchmark model performance given our objective of extrapolating from single to higher order mutations.
The campaign for cinchocaine was more difficult to navigate and we failed to observe beneficial mutations beyond a double mutant, despite taking multiple ISM paths (Fig. S14).
This result (i.e., running into dead ends during ISM) supported the need to include ML models in our framework that might capture epistatic interactions.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ISM failure for cinchocaine and availability of HSS single mutant data.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to predict higher-order mutants that capture epistatic interactions using only single mutant data?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Augmented ridge regression models using single mutant data and zero-shot fitness predictions can extrapolate to higher-order mutants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design augmented ridge regression ML models with variant features concatenating site-specific amino acid encodings and zero-shot fitness predictions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Model architecture designed to leverage single mutant data for predicting higher-order mutants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Train and evaluate the models retrospectively on moclobemide and metoclopramide data to select the best configuration.

evidence.quote_or_span: The key idea was to use single mutant data from the HSS to extrapolate higher order mutants with increased activity (Fig. 3A).

To achieve this goal, we chose to fit augmented ridge regression models with our data—as such models are simple and have been previously shown to outperform more sophisticated models for protein engineering
McbA variant feature representations consisted of site-specific amino acid encodings concatenated with a zero-shot fitness prediction
Ridge regression models were augmented following the code accompanying the elegant work of Hsu et al.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which zero-shot predictor and encoding strategy yields the best predictive performance?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: EVmutation with Georgiev encodings will provide the best augmentation for ridge regression.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train and evaluate ML models retrospectively on moclobemide and metoclopramide data, using NDCG and Spearman correlation to select best zero-shot predictor (EVmutation) and encoding (Georgiev).
evolution_trajectory.observation: Augmented models outperformed ridge regression alone; EVmutation with Georgiev encodings selected as best configuration.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze whether full site saturation data (n=77) provides more predictive power than reduced libraries.

evidence.quote_or_span: Training and hyperparameter tuning were performed using single mutant data (n=77) from the HSS (top four hot spots; Fig. 3B, C).
The augmented models outperformed the ridge regression model alone when evaluated with NDCG.
Model evaluation and selection were first performed retrospectively by using the assay-labeled datasets from our moclobemide and metoclopramide engineering campaigns.
Spearman correlation coefficient and NDCG were used to select the best zero-shot predictor and encoding strategy, with a preference given to NDCG.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does using the full site saturation dataset (n=77) improve predictive power compared to reduced libraries?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Full site saturation data provides more predictive power than reduced libraries.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare model performance using full site saturation dataset vs. reduced libraries; confirm augmented models outperform ridge regression alone.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Full site saturation dataset (n=77) provides more predictive power; augmented models outperform ridge regression alone.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to experimentally test top ML predictions for the three target reactions.

evidence.quote_or_span: this analysis indicated that utilizing all the data gathered from the site saturation dataset provides more predictive power
we decided to use the site saturation dataset and the augmented EVmutation model with Georgiev encodings given the strong predictive performance
Training and hyperparameter tuning were performed using single mutant data (n=77) from the HSS (top four hot spots; Fig. 3B, C).

This can likely be attributed to the nature of the rich datasets mostly containing mutants with non-zero activity (64/77 for moclobemide and 62/77 for metoclopramide), preventing “holes” in training sets $^{14}$ .

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ML model trained and validated retrospectively.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the ML model predict higher-order mutants with improved activity for moclobemide, metoclopramide, and cinchocaine?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Top 25 ML predictions will be enriched in high-activity variants, some surpassing ISM quadruple mutants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Experimentally test top 25 ML predictions for moclobemide, metoclopramide, and cinchocaine.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ML-predicted variants enriched in high activity; some surpassed ISM quadruple mutants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze specific mutants to understand how ML outperforms ISM.

evidence.quote_or_span: the augmented ML model was able to predict McbA variants enriched in high activity for each amide product when tested experimentally, some even surpassing qm-McbA from both moclobemide and metoclopramide ISM campaigns

3 mM NADH, 50 mM 4-chlorobenzoic acid, 15 U/mL pyruvate kinase and lactate dehydrogenase enzyme mix (Sigma-Aldrich P0294), 25 U/mL myokinase (Sigma-Aldrich 475941), and various concentrations (50–200 μg/mL) of the studied McbA variant.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Experimental validation of ML predictions completed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why did ML predictions outperform ISM in some cases?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ML models can identify superior mutants overlooked by ISM and avoid path dependencies.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze specific mutants (e.g., A424S for metoclopramide, A205L for cinchocaine) to understand ML advantages.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ML model identified superior mutants overlooked by ISM (e.g., A424S for metoclopramide) and avoided path dependencies (e.g., A205L for cinchocaine).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply the ML-guided framework to six additional pharmaceutical compounds to test generalization.

evidence.quote_or_span: the best predicted mutant for metoclopramide contained a mutation (A424S) that was superseded in the HSS by a more active mutation (A424T) carried forward in ISM, indicating the model found a superior mutant that would have been overlooked using ISM alone

The best predicted variant for cinchocaine had significantly higher activity than the best single mutation on its own and surprisingly contained a mutation (A205L) that decreased activity compared to wt-McbA in the HSS

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ML-guided framework validated on initial three targets.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the ML-guided framework generalize to other pharmaceutical compounds?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The framework can be applied to new targets by selecting hot spots, training ML models, and testing top predictions.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Apply ML-guided framework to six additional pharmaceutical compounds: select four hot spots, train ML model, test top 24 predictions, and compare best variant to wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Best predicted mutant always outperformed best rational design, with 1.6- to 34-fold improvement over wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Identify limitations of the approach, including screening bottlenecks and low signal-to-noise issues.

evidence.quote_or_span: For each compound, the best predicted mutant always outperformed the best rational design
select four hot spots, and train our ML model to predict higher order mutants with increased activity
For each reaction, the top 24 predictions were tested, and the best variant was expressed, purified, and compared to wt-McbA activity.
We observed increases in yields ranging from 1.6-fold to 34-fold over wt-McbA for the six compounds we tested

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Framework successfully applied to six additional compounds.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the limitations of the current approach?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Screening remains a bottleneck, and low signal-to-noise in HSS can lead to flat fitness landscapes that are difficult to model.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitations: screening bottleneck (LC-MS throughput may not suit all enzymes), and some mutants show modest improvements due to low signal-to-noise in HSS.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Screening remains a bottleneck; LC-MS provides manageable solution but may not suit all enzymes. Some mutants show modest improvements due to low signal-to-noise in HSS.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: These limitations inform future improvements to the platform.

evidence.quote_or_span: Some mutants gave only modest improvements, which may be an artifact of low signal-to-noise in the hot spot screens for some of the target compounds that were only detectable by MS. This can lead to flat fitness landscapes that are more difficult to model.
experimental screening methods for biocatalytic reactions remain a bottleneck
LC-MS provided a manageable solution, as has been found in other examples

validation_methods: Experimental testing of top ML predictions
Application to six additional pharmaceutical compounds
success_verification.final_conclusion: The ML-guided cell-free platform successfully engineered McbA variants with improved activity for multiple amide products, outperforming traditional ISM in several cases and demonstrating generalization to new targets.
limitations: Screening remains a bottleneck; LC-MS throughput may not suit all enzymes
Low signal-to-noise in HSS can lead to flat fitness landscapes and modest improvements
Model rank correlation with experimental results is low (average Spearman's 0.21 ± 0.15), indicating incomplete capture of sequence-fitness landscape
